title: Kashmiri Dum Aloo

ingredients.name: small potatoes (golf-ball size), boiled and peeled

subs.name: baby potatoes or cubed large potatoes
subs.notes: fry cubed potatoes for only 4 minutes; piercing is still important

subs.name: 100 ml full-fat coconut milk for a dairy-free version
subs.notes: add all at once rather than spoon by spoon; stir gently to avoid splitting

ingredients.name: Kashmiri red chilli powder
visual_checks: Kashmiri mirch gives colour without fierce heat
ingredients.notes: Kashmiri mirch gives colour without fierce heat

subs.name: ghee
subs.notes: ghee gives a richer, milder base; use the same quantity

steps.text: Pierce each boiled potato all over with a fork or toothpick — this allows the gravy to penetrate the skin. Mix the Kashmiri chilli powder with 1 tbsp of water to form a paste and set aside.

Heat mustard oil in a kadai over high flame until pale. Deep-fry or shallow-fry the pierced potatoes in batches, turning, until the skins are puckered and golden-brown, about 6 minutes per batch. Remove and drain.

Reduce oil in the same kadai to 3 tbsp. Add the cloves, cardamom and a pinch of asafoetida over medium heat. Let them sizzle for 20 seconds. Add the chilli paste and stir for 1 minute — the oil will turn brilliant red.
Add the whisked curd one tablespoon at a time, stirring vigorously after each addition to prevent splitting. Continue adding until all the curd is in; the sauce should look creamy and rust-red.
Add the ground fennel, dried ginger powder and turmeric. Stir to combine, then pour in 200 ml water and season with salt. Bring to a simmer.

Add the fried potatoes to the gravy. Cover tightly and cook on low for 20 minutes (dum cooking) — the potatoes will absorb the red sauce and swell. Lift the lid and check the gravy consistency; it should coat the potatoes thickly.
Taste for salt and serve with steamed basmati rice. The potatoes will have turned a deep mahogany-red and should be yielding all the way through when squeezed gently.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: mustard-oil → ghee (ghee gives a richer, milder base; use the same quantity); curd → 100 ml full-fat coconut milk for a dairy-free version (add all at once rather than spoon by spoon; stir gently to avoid splitting); potato → baby potatoes or cubed large potatoes (fry cubed potatoes for only 4 minutes; piercing is still important)
